title: Wash Produce
summary: Clean fruits and vegetables properly before eating or cooking.
learnerPromise: You will be able to wash fruits and vegetables thoroughly to remove dirt and reduce surface contaminants before eating or cooking.
whyItMatters: Fresh produce can carry dirt, bacteria, or residue on its surface. Washing reduces the chance of illness and makes food safer to eat raw or cooked.
realLifeUses: Washing apples before packing them for lunch
Rinsing potatoes before baking or boiling
Washing berries before eating them as a snack
result: Clean produce that is ready to eat or cook.
youWillLearn: Rinse produce under cool running water
Use a brush on firm-skinned items like potatoes or carrots
Dry produce with a clean towel to remove extra moisture
Know which produce needs extra attention and which is pre-washed
miniChallenge: Wash three different fruits or vegetables from your kitchen using the proper method for each type.
steps: Start with clean hands and a clean sink area.
Hold the produce under cool running water. Rub the surface gently with your fingers.
For firm produce like potatoes or cucumbers, use a clean brush to scrub the surface.
For leafy greens, separate the leaves and rinse each one individually.
Pat dry with a clean towel or paper towels. Use right away or store properly.
completionCriteria: All visible dirt is removed from the produce
You rinsed every item under running water before eating or cooking
You dried the produce or used it promptly after washing
commonProblems: Washing produce with soap, which can leave residue
Not drying greens well, making salad dressing slide off
Washing berries too roughly and crushing them
tips: Wash produce right before you eat it, not when you bring it home—extra moisture can speed spoilage.
Do not use soap, bleach, or commercial produce washes. Cool running water is enough.
Pre-washed bagged salads do not need to be washed again unless the package says to.
